title: RBA still stuck between a rock and a hike place
full_text:
The latest jobs data delivered a little of what the Reserve Bank needs. But whether it’s enough to prevent at least one more interest rate hike remains to be seen.
As this column has been arguing for the last few months, the RBA’s inflation fighting efforts will be made harder by two key factors: the enormous boom in household wealth in the past two years (about $3 trillion all up), and the fact that spending driven by structural changes in the economy (such as investment in data centres, the energy transition and the critical minerals sector) will continue almost irrespective of where interest rates get to.
So governor Michele Bullock and her rate-setting board have little choice but to strangle Australian households with rate rises to slow the economy, bring house prices down and weaken the jobs market.
On Thursday, we saw a bit of progress on the latter, when the official employment numbers showed the economy shed 18,600 jobs in April, lifting the underemployment rate to 4.5 per cent from 4.3 per cent.
To be sure, it’s a small move in a nation of almost 28 million people. But it was a surprise, given that economists had expected employment to grow by about 15,000. And it may be evidence that the combined effect of the three consecutive rate rises and the energy shock created by the war in Iran is arriving sooner than expected.
There’s good news and bad news in that.
The good news is, as Betashares chief economist David Bassanese put it, that the RBA “can and should take a June leave pass” and leave interest rates on hold when it meets next month.
The economy has had an awful lot thrown at it in the last few months, and Bullock and her staff have a bit of wriggle room to see how the data is shaping up over June and July.
For one thing, the jobs market will require closer scrutiny. The fall in employment numbers and the rise in the unemployment rate suggest the labour market has weakened. But the unemployment rate actually fell in April, raising a red flag that the central bank will want to keep an eye on.
The good news that rates will likely remain on hold for at least 10 weeks must be tempered by two factors. The first is that the inflation threat has not passed. The Strait of Hormuz remains all but shut, oil prices remain elevated and physical fuel supplies are under ever-growing pressure.
The base case for the RBA’s growth and inflation outlook is predicated on the vague assumption that the war in Iran will end “soon”, but confidence in that must be ebbing on the RBA board. We are many months away from returning to pre-war traffic levels in the strait, and both hard inflation data and anecdotal evidence, including from retail giants Coles and Woolworths, indicate price rises are ahead of us.
It is too early to take an August rate rise off the table, and the potential for two more increases is very real, particularly if you believe the transmission effect of rate movements has been blunted by Australia’s massive household wealth boom.
The most obvious bad news is that the fall in employment and rise in the jobless rate are signs that the economy is losing momentum. This is, as Morgan Stanley’s equity strategist Chris Nicol said this week, the slowdown we have to have if we want to tame inflation, but that doesn’t make it any more fun, particularly for investors with exposure to the domestic economy.
Consumer discretionary stocks, housing related stocks and healthcare stocks can expect more selling pressure, and rising unemployment is more bad news for a banking sector that is processing the sudden hit to property investing for the federal budget.
The ASX 200 rose strongly on Thursday as investors welcomed whispers from Washington about peace talks with Iran, and money markets bet that we’ll only see the RBA rise once more this year, not twice.
But local investors can’t have it both ways. The outlook for the domestic economy and domestic earnings did not improve today. Quite the opposite.
YXJ0aWNsZTo3YjdlOTA2My1lMDMwLTQ0NjMtOGIyMS0xMWY4OTgzMWNiZTI
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Thomson
heading: Banking and Economy
